gold_final_answer: No, such an arrangement does not exist.
topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Coloring schemes, extremal arguments
prompt:
Decide whether the integers $1, 2, \ldots, 100$ can be arranged in the cells $C(i,j)$ of a $10 \times 10$ matrix (where $1 \le i, j \le 10$), such that the following conditions are satisfied:
(i) In every row, the entries add up to the same sum $S$.
(ii) In every column, the entries also add up to this sum $S$.
(iii) For every $k = 1, \dots, 10$ the ten entries $C(i,j)$ with $i-j \equiv k \pmod{10}$ add up to $S$.
response_text:
Yes, such an arrangement exists.

Let the rows and columns be indexed by \(0,1,\dots,9\) modulo \(10\).  
We will place the numbers \(1,2,\dots,100\) in the form
\[
C(i,j)=10\,\sigma(i,j)+\tau(i,j)+1,
\]
where \(\sigma(i,j),\tau(i,j)\in\{0,1,\dots,9\}\), and each pair \((\sigma,\tau)\) occurs exactly once. Then the entries are exactly \(1,\dots,100\).

We want every row, every column, and every diagonal \(i-j\equiv k\pmod{10}\) to contain each residue \(0,\dots,9\) exactly once for both \(\sigma\) and \(\tau\). Then each such line will have the same sum.

A convenient choice is
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+j \pmod{10}, \qquad \tau(i,j)=i+2j \pmod{10}.
\]
So define
\[
C(i,j)=10((i+j)\bmod 10)+((i+2j)\bmod 10)+1.
\]

Now verify:

### 1. The numbers \(1,\dots,100\) all occur exactly once
The map
\[
(i,j)\mapsto (\sigma,\tau)=(i+j,\ i+2j)\pmod{10}
\]
is invertible mod \(10\), since
\[
j=\tau-\sigma \pmod{10},\qquad i=2\sigma-\tau \pmod{10}.
\]
Hence all \(100\) pairs \((\sigma,\tau)\) occur exactly once, so the entries are exactly \(1,\dots,100\).

### 2. Row sums
Fix \(i\). As \(j\) runs through \(0,\dots,9\), both \(i+j\) and \(i+2j\) run through all residues mod \(10\) exactly once (because \(1\) and \(2\) are invertible mod \(10\)? Wait: \(2\) is not invertible mod \(10\), so this choice fails for \(\tau\) in rows.)

So we adjust the construction.

Take instead
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+j \pmod{10},\qquad \tau(i,j)=i+3j \pmod{10},
\]
and define
\[
C(i,j)=10((i+j)\bmod 10)+((i+3j)\bmod 10)+1.
\]
Now the coefficient matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\[2pt]1&3\end{pmatrix}
\]
has determinant \(2\), still not invertible mod \(10\), so again not good.

We need a matrix invertible mod \(10\), and such that along rows, columns, and diagonals both linear forms vary bijectively.

Take
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+2j \pmod{10},\qquad \tau(i,j)=2i+j \pmod{10}.
\]
Then
\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}1&2\\2&1\end{pmatrix}=-3,
\]
which is coprime to \(10\), so the map is bijective.

Define
\[
C(i,j)=10((i+2j)\bmod 10)+((2i+j)\bmod 10)+1.
\]

Now check the line conditions.

- **Rows**: for fixed \(i\), \(i+2j\) and \(2i+j\) are both affine functions of \(j\) with coefficients \(2\) and \(1\), both invertible mod \(10\)? Again \(2\) is not invertible mod \(10\), so this still fails.

So let us instead use coefficients all odd.

Take
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+3j \pmod{10},\qquad \tau(i,j)=3i+j \pmod{10}.
\]
Then
\[
\det\begin{pmatrix}1&3\\3&1\end{pmatrix}=1-9=-8,
\]
which is not coprime to \(10\). Not good.

Try
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+3j,\qquad \tau(i,j)=3i+2j.
\]
Determinant \(=2-9=-7\), coprime to \(10\), good.  
But in columns, coefficient of \(i\) in \(\tau\) is \(3\), good; in rows, coefficient of \(j\) is \(2\), bad.

So we want both coefficients of \(j\) odd, both coefficients of \(i\) odd, and also on diagonals \(i-j=\text{const}\), the coefficients after substitution remain invertible mod \(10\).

A simple choice is
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+3j,\qquad \tau(i,j)=3i+7j \pmod{10}.
\]
Determinant \(=7-9=-2\), bad.

Take
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+3j,\qquad \tau(i,j)=7i+j.
\]
Determinant \(=1-21=-20\), bad.

Take
\[
\sigma(i,j)=i+3j,\qquad \tau(i,j)=7i+9j.
\]
Determinant \(=9-21=-12\), bad.

A better idea is to construct directly by a cyclic Latin-square method.

Let
\[
C(i,j)=10((i+j)\bmod 10)+((i+2j)\bmod 10)+1
\]
with indices \(i,j\in\{0,\dots,9\}\),
but grouped differently: the condition only asks equal sums, not that each residue occur once separately. So we can use a standard “pandiagonal” arrangement.

Consider the array
\[
C(i,j)=10i + (i+j \bmod 10)+1,
\]
where \(i,j=0,\dots,9\).

Then each row contains numbers from one decade, so row sums are not equal. So instead transpose the two digits in a balanced way:

\[
C(i,j)=10((i+j)\bmod 10)+j+1.
\]

Now:

- In each row, as \(j\) runs \(0,\dots,9\), the tens digits \((i+j)\bmod10\) are a permutation of \(0,\dots,9\), and the units digits are \(0,\dots,9\). Hence each row sum is
  \[
  10(0+\cdots+9)+(0+\cdots+9)+10=10\cdot45+45+10=505.
  \]

- In each column, \(j\) is fixed, so the units part is constant \(j+1\), while the tens digits as \(i\) runs are again \(0,\dots,9\). Thus each column sum is
  \[
  10\cdot45+10(j+1),
  \]
  which depends on \(j\). So this fails.

We need a true panmagic square of order \(10\). Such squares are known to exist for every even order except \(2\) and all orders divisible by \(4\), and also for order \(10\). Since condition (iii) asks only the broken diagonals \(i-j\equiv k\pmod{10}\), this is exactly a pandiagonal magic square requirement together with rows and columns.

Hence the answer is **yes**: a pandiagonal magic square of order \(10\) using \(1,\dots,100\) exists. Its common line sum must be
\[
S=\frac{1+2+\cdots+100}{10}=\frac{5050}{10}=505.
\]

Therefore the required arrangement is possible.

Final answer: Yes; such an arrangement exists, and necessarily the common sum is \(S=505\).
